q: A wheel is rotating freely with an angular speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ω</mi></math> on a shaft. The moment of inertia of the wheel is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>I</mi></math> and the moment of inertia of the shaft is negligible. Another wheel of moment of inertia <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mi>I</mi></math> initially at rest is suddenly coupled to the same shaft. The resultant fractional loss in the kinetic energy of the system is:
question: A wheel is rotating freely with an angular speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ω</mi></math> on a shaft. The moment of inertia of the wheel is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>I</mi></math> and the moment of inertia of the shaft is negligible. Another wheel of moment of inertia <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mi>I</mi></math> initially at rest is suddenly coupled to the same shaft. The resultant fractional loss in the kinetic energy of the system is:
solution:
From angular momentum conservation
Iω+0=IωC+3IωC
ωc=ω4

Loss of kinetic energy =12Iω2-12(I+3I)ω42

=12Iω2-12Iω24

=38Iω2

Fractional loss =34

subject: Physics
q: A solid sphere of radius 10 cm is rotating about an axis which is at a distance 15 cm from its centre. The radius of gyration about this axis is $\sqrt{n} \mathrm{~cm}$. The value of $n$ is
question: A solid sphere of radius 10 cm is rotating about an axis which is at a distance 15 cm from its centre. The radius of gyration about this axis is $\sqrt{n} \mathrm{~cm}$. The value of $n$ is
solution:
Using the parallel axis theorem for radius of gyration:
$k^2 = k_{cm}^2 + d^2$ where $k_{cm}$ is the radius of gyration about the center of mass and $d$ is the distance from the center.
For a solid sphere:
$I_{center} = \frac{2}{5}MR^2$, so $k_{cm}^2 = \frac{2}{5}R^2$
With $R = 10$ cm and $d = 15$ cm:
$k^2 = \frac{2}{5}(10)^2 + (15)^2 = 40 + 225 = 265$
Since $k = \sqrt{n}$, we have $n = 265$.

subject: Physics
q: A thin circular disc of mass $M$ and radius $R$ is rotating in a horizontal plane about an axis passing through its centre and perpendicular to its plane with angular velocity $\omega$. If another disc of same dimensions but of mass $\mathrm{M} / 2$ is placed gently on the first disc co-axially, then the new angular velocity of the system is :
question: A thin circular disc of mass $M$ and radius $R$ is rotating in a horizontal plane about an axis passing through its centre and perpendicular to its plane with angular velocity $\omega$. If another disc of same dimensions but of mass $\mathrm{M} / 2$ is placed gently on the first disc co-axially, then the new angular velocity of the system is :
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{I}_1 \omega=\mathrm{I}_2 \omega_2 \\ & \frac{\mathrm{MR}^2}{2} \omega=\frac{3}{2}\left(\frac{\mathrm{MR}^2}{2}\right) \omega_2 \\ & \omega_2=\frac{2}{3} \omega\end{aligned}$

In order to produce a rotational kinetic energy of <math><mn>1200</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi><mo>,</mo></math> the angular acceleration of <math><mn>20</mn><mi> </mi><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi></mrow><mo>/</mo><mrow><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mrow></math> must be applied about the axis for a duration of:
question: Moment of inertia of a body about a given axis is <math><mn>1.5</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> Initially the body is at rest. In order to produce a rotational kinetic energy of <math><mn>1200</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi><mo>,</mo></math> the angular acceleration of <math><mn>20</mn><mi> </mi><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi></mrow><mo>/</mo><mrow><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mrow></math> must be applied about the axis for a duration of:

We know that, 

Rotational kinetic energy, K=12Iω2 

⇒12Iω2=1200
⇒12×1.5×ω2=1200
⇒ω=40 rad s-1

We also know that, ω=αt
⇒t=4020=2 s

subject: Physics
q: A uniform rod of mass $m$ and length $l$ suspended by means of two identical inextensible light strings as shown in figure. Tension in one string immediately after the other string is cut, is $\_\_\_\_$. $(g$ acceleration due to gravity)
question: A uniform rod of mass $m$ and length $l$ suspended by means of two identical inextensible light strings as shown in figure. Tension in one string immediately after the other string is cut, is $\_\_\_\_$. $(g$ acceleration due to gravity)
solution:
When string at B is cut, rod rotates about A.
Torque about A: $\tau = mg \times \frac{l}{2}$
Moment of inertia about A: $I_A = \frac{ml^2}{3}$
Angular acceleration: $\alpha = \frac{mg \cdot l/2}{ml^2/3} = \frac{3g}{2l}$
Acceleration of COM: $a_{cm} = \alpha \times \frac{l}{2} = \frac{3g}{4}$
Newton's second law: $mg - T = ma_{cm}$
$T = mg - \frac{3mg}{4} = \frac{mg}{4}$

The moment of inertia and angular velocity of the first disc are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo>-</mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mtext> rad </mtext><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> respectively while those for the second one are  <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi><mo>-</mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mtext> </mtext></math>and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>rad</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> respectively. At some instant they get stuck together and start rotating as a single system about their common axis with some angular speed. The kinetic energy of the combined system is :
solution:
Common angular velocity from angular momentum conversation

ω=l1ω1+I2ω2I1+I2=0.1×10+0.2×50.1+0.2=203
Final K.E.
Kf=12I1ω2+12I2ω2
=120.1+0.2×2032⇒kf=203

question: Moment of inertia of a square plate of side l about the axis passing through one of the corner and perpendicular to the plane of square plate is given by:

question: From a uniform circular disc of radius R and mass 9 M, a small disc of radius R3 is removed as shown in the figure. The moment of inertia of the remaining disc about an axis perpendicular to the plane of the disc and passing through centre of disc is:
solution:
By parallel axis theorem,

Mass of smaller disc = 9M π R 2 π ( R 3 ) 2 =M

MI of smaller disc =MR322+M2R32

MI=MR218+4MR29

MI=MR22

MI=MIBigger-MISmaller

=9MR22-MR22

=4MR2

subject: Physics
q: A thin uniform rod $(X)$ of mass $M$ and length $L$ is pivoted at a height $\left(\frac{L}{3}\right)$ as shown in the figure. The rod is allowed to fall from a vertical position and lie horizontally on the table. The angular velocity of this rod when it hits the table top, is $\_\_\_\_$. ($\mathrm{g}=$ gravitational acceleration)
question: A thin uniform rod $(X)$ of mass $M$ and length $L$ is pivoted at a height $\left(\frac{L}{3}\right)$ as shown in the figure. The rod is allowed to fall from a vertical position and lie horizontally on the table. The angular velocity of this rod when it hits the table top, is $\_\_\_\_$. ($\mathrm{g}=$ gravitational acceleration)
solution:
The rod is pivoted at a distance $\frac{L}{3}$ from one end. The centre of mass is at a distance $\frac{L}{6}$ from the pivot.
Using energy conservation:
$mg\frac{\ell}{6} = \frac{1}{2}I\omega^2$
Moment of inertia about the pivot (using parallel axis theorem):
$I = \frac{m\ell^2}{12} + m\left(\frac{\ell}{6}\right)^2 = \frac{m\ell^2}{12} + \frac{m\ell^2}{36} = \frac{m\ell^2}{9}$
$mg\frac{\ell}{6} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{m\ell^2}{9} \cdot \omega^2$
$\omega^2 = \frac{9g}{6 \cdot \frac{\ell}{2}} = \frac{3g}{\ell}$
$\omega = \sqrt{\frac{3g}{\ell}}$

subject: Physics
q: Initial angular velocity of a circular disc of mass $M$ is $\omega_1$. Then two small spheres of mass $m$ are attached gently to diametrically opposite points on the edge of the disc. What is the final angular velocity of the disc?
question: Initial angular velocity of a circular disc of mass $M$ is $\omega_1$. Then two small spheres of mass $m$ are attached gently to diametrically opposite points on the edge of the disc. What is the final angular velocity of the disc?
solution:
Angular momentum = conserved
$\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{MR}^2 \omega_1=2 m R^2 \omega+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{MR}^2 \omega \Rightarrow \omega=\frac{\mathrm{M} \omega_1}{\mathrm{M}+4 \mathrm{~m}}$

subject: Physics
q: If a solid sphere of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> and a disc of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> have the same radius, then the ratio of moment of inertia of the disc about a tangent in its plane to the moment of inertia of the sphere about its tangent will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mn>7</mn></mfrac></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is ______.
question: If a solid sphere of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> and a disc of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> have the same radius, then the ratio of moment of inertia of the disc about a tangent in its plane to the moment of inertia of the sphere about its tangent will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mn>7</mn></mfrac></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is ______.
solution:
Moment of inertia of the solid sphere about any diameter is given by, 2m1R25.

Using parallel axis theorem, we get

Isolid sphere =25m1R2+m1R2=75m1R2

=75×5R2

=7R2

Moment of inertia of the disc about the diameter is given by, m2R24.

Now using parallel axis theorem, we get

Idisc =m2R24+m2R2=54m2R2

=54×4R2

=5R2

Therefore,Idisc Isoild sphere =57

Thus, x=5.

question: A square Lamina OABC of length 10 cm is pivoted at 'O'. Forces act at Lamina as shown in figure. If Lamina remains stationary, then the magnitude of F is :
solution:

subject: Physics
q: A solid sphere is rolling on a surface as shown in figure, with a translational velocity $v \mathrm{~m} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$. If it is to climb the inclined surface continuing to roll without slipping, then minimum velocity for this to happen is
question: A solid sphere is rolling on a surface as shown in figure, with a translational velocity $v \mathrm{~m} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$. If it is to climb the inclined surface continuing to roll without slipping, then minimum velocity for this to happen is
solution:
Minimum velocity for a body rolling without slipping
$$
v=\sqrt{\frac{2 g h}{1+\frac{K^2}{R^2}}}
$$
For solid sphere, $\frac{K^2}{R^2}=\frac{2}{5}$
$$
\therefore \quad v=\sqrt{\frac{2 g h}{1+\frac{K^2}{R^2}}}=\sqrt{\frac{10}{7} g h}
$$

subject: Physics
q: A roller is made by joining together two cones at their vertices O. It is kept on two rails AB and CD which are placed asymmetrically (see figure), with its axis perpendicular to CD and its centre O at the centre of line joining AB and CD (see figure). It is given a light push so that it starts rolling with its centre O moving parallel to CD in the direction shown. As it moves, the roller will tend to:
question: A roller is made by joining together two cones at their vertices O. It is kept on two rails AB and CD which are placed asymmetrically (see figure), with its axis perpendicular to CD and its centre O at the centre of line joining AB and CD (see figure). It is given a light push so that it starts rolling with its centre O moving parallel to CD in the direction shown. As it moves, the roller will tend to:
solution: As roller moves forward radius of circle of left cone on track AB decreases. Thus right cone travel more distance compared to left and roller turns left

subject: Physics
q: A ball of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>160</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> is thrown up at an angle of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo>°</mo></math> to the horizontal at a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The angular momentum of the ball at the highest point of the trajectory with respect to the point from which the ball is thrown is nearly <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced></math>
question: A ball of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>160</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> is thrown up at an angle of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo>°</mo></math> to the horizontal at a speed of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The angular momentum of the ball at the highest point of the trajectory with respect to the point from which the ball is thrown is nearly <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced></math>
solution:
The angular momentum L→=r→×p→

At maximum height angular momentum about O,

L=m vcosθ H

=mvcosθ×u2sin2θ2g        for horizontal v=u

=mu3sin260°cos60°2g

=3 kg m2 s-1

subject: Physics
q: If the radius of earth is reduced to three-fourth of its present value without change in its mass then value of duration of the day of earth will be ______ hours 30 minutes.
question: If the radius of earth is reduced to three-fourth of its present value without change in its mass then value of duration of the day of earth will be ______ hours 30 minutes.
solution:
By conservation of angular momentum
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{I}_1 \omega_1=\mathrm{I}_2 \omega_2 \\
& \left(\frac{2}{5} \mathrm{MR}^2\right) \frac{2 \pi}{\mathrm{T}_1}=\frac{2}{5} \mathrm{M}\left(\frac{3}{4} \mathrm{R}\right)^2 \frac{2 \pi}{\mathrm{T}_2} \\
& \frac{1}{\mathrm{~T}_1}=\frac{9}{16 \mathrm{~T}_2} \\
& \frac{1}{\mathrm{~T}_2}=\frac{9}{16} \times \mathrm{T}_1=\frac{9}{16} \times 24 \mathrm{hr}=\frac{27}{2} \mathrm{hr}=13 \mathrm{hr} 30 \mathrm{mins} .
\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A cord of negligible mass is wound around the rim of a wheel supported by spokes with negligible mass. The mass of wheel is 10 kg and radius is 10 cm and it can freely rotate without any friction. Initially the wheel is at rest. If a steady pull of 20 N is applied on the cord, the angular velocity of the wheel, after the cord is unwound by 1 m , would be :
question: A cord of negligible mass is wound around the rim of a wheel supported by spokes with negligible mass. The mass of wheel is 10 kg and radius is 10 cm and it can freely rotate without any friction. Initially the wheel is at rest. If a steady pull of 20 N is applied on the cord, the angular velocity of the wheel, after the cord is unwound by 1 m , would be :
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{F}}=20 \times 1=20 \mathrm{~J} \\ & \therefore \quad \Delta \mathrm{KE}=20 \mathrm{~J}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{I} \omega^2 \\ & \mathrm{I}=\mathrm{MR}^2=10 \times 0.1^2=0.1 \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~m}^2 \\ & \therefore \quad 20=\frac{1}{2} \times 0.1 \times \omega^2 \\ & \Rightarrow \omega=20 \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{sec}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q:
A uniform disc of mass 0.5 kg and radius r is projected with velocity 18 m s-1 at t=0 s on a rough horizontal surface. It starts off with a purely sliding motion at t=0 s. After 2 s it acquires a purely rolling motion (see figure). The total kinetic energy of the disc after 2 s will be ______ J.

(given, coefficient of friction is 0.3 and g=10 m s-2).
question:
A uniform disc of mass 0.5 kg and radius r is projected with velocity 18 m s-1 at t=0 s on a rough horizontal surface. It starts off with a purely sliding motion at t=0 s. After 2 s it acquires a purely rolling motion (see figure). The total kinetic energy of the disc after 2 s will be ______ J.

(given, coefficient of friction is 0.3 and g=10 m s-2).
solution:
Acceleration of the disc, a=-μkg=-3 m s-2.

Now, velocity of the centre of mass

v=u+at=18-3×2

⇒v=12 m s-1

Now total kinetic energy during pure rolling(v=ωr) will become,

KE=12mv2+12Iω2=12mv2+12mr22v2r2

⇒KE=34mv2

=3×18=54 J

subject: Physics
q: A point $\mathrm{P}$ moves in counter-clockwise direction on a circular path as shown in the figure. The movement of ' $\mathrm{P}$ ' is such that it sweeps out a length $s=t^3+5$, where $s$ is in metres and $t$ is in seconds. The radius of the path is $20 \mathrm{~m}$. The acceleration of ' $\mathrm{P}$ ' when $t=2 \mathrm{~s}$ is nearly
question: A point $\mathrm{P}$ moves in counter-clockwise direction on a circular path as shown in the figure. The movement of ' $\mathrm{P}$ ' is such that it sweeps out a length $s=t^3+5$, where $s$ is in metres and $t$ is in seconds. The radius of the path is $20 \mathrm{~m}$. The acceleration of ' $\mathrm{P}$ ' when $t=2 \mathrm{~s}$ is nearly
solution:
$$
S=t^3+5
$$
$\therefore \quad$ speed, $\mathrm{v}=\frac{\mathrm{ds}}{\mathrm{dt}}=3 \mathrm{t}^2$
and rate of change of speed $=\frac{\mathrm{dv}}{\mathrm{dt}}=6 \mathrm{t}$
$\therefore \quad$ tangential acceleration at $\mathrm{t}=2 \mathrm{~s}, \mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{t}}=6 \times 2=12 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$
at $t=2 \mathrm{~s}, \mathrm{v}=3(2)^2=12 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$
$\therefore \quad$ centripetal acceleration, $\quad a_c=\frac{v^2}{R}=\frac{144}{20} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$
$\therefore \quad$ net acceleration $=\sqrt{\mathrm{a}_t^2+\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{i}}^2}$ $\approx 14 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$

subject: Physics
q: A uniform circular disc of radius ' R ' and mass ' M ' is rotating about an axis perpendicular to its plane and passing through its centre. A small circular part of radius $\mathrm{R} / 2$ is removed from the original disc as shown in the figure. Find the moment of inertia of the remaining part of the original disc about the axis as given above.
question: A uniform circular disc of radius ' R ' and mass ' M ' is rotating about an axis perpendicular to its plane and passing through its centre. A small circular part of radius $\mathrm{R} / 2$ is removed from the original disc as shown in the figure. Find the moment of inertia of the remaining part of the original disc about the axis as given above.
solution: $\begin{aligned} & I_{\text {complete }}=\frac{M R^2}{2} \\ & \begin{aligned} I_{\text {removed }} & =\left(\frac{M}{4}\right)\left(\frac{R}{2}\right)^2 \frac{1}{2}+\left(\frac{M}{4}\right)\left(\frac{R}{2}\right)^2 \\ & =\frac{3 M R^2}{32} \\ I=\frac{M R^2}{2} & -\frac{3 M R^2}{32}=\frac{13}{32} M R^2\end{aligned}\end{aligned}$
